examples.title: Algorithm: IoT Weather Monitoring Data Flow
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating the complete data flow from sensor collection to cloud visualization in an IoT weather monitoring system
examples.code:
ALGORITHM WeatherMonitoringSystem
INPUT: Physical weather parameters from sensors
OUTPUT: Visualized weather data on dashboard

BEGIN
 // Phase 1: Sensor Data Collection
 sensors[] <- INITIALIZE_SENSORS(temperature, humidity, pressure, wind_speed)
 
 FOR each sampling_interval DO
 FOR each sensor IN sensors[] DO
 raw_data <- sensor.READ_VALUE()
 sensor_data[SensorID] <- raw_data
 END FOR
 
 // Phase 2: Data Processing at Microcontroller
 processed_data <- MICROCONTROLLER.PROCESS(sensor_data)
 processed_data.timestamp <- GET_CURRENT_TIMESTAMP()
 processed_data.location <- GET_GPS_LOCATION()
 
 // Phase 3: Data Transmission via Protocol
 connection <- ESTABLISH_CONNECTION(protocol="MQTT", server=cloud_server)
 packet <- PACKAGE_DATA(processed_data)
 TRANSMIT(packet, connection)
 
 // Phase 4: Cloud Data Storage
 CLOUD_STORE.insert(processed_data)
 
 // Phase 5: Data Analysis
 IF ANALYZE_WEATHER_PATTERN(processed_data) THEN
 ALERT <- GENERATE_WEATHER_ALERT(processed_data)
 NOTIFY_USERS(ALERT)
 END IF
 
 // Phase 6: Data Visualization
 DASHBOARD_UPDATE <- FORMAT_FOR_VISUALIZATION(processed_data)
 DISPLAY(DASHBOARD_UPDATE)
 END FOR
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the complete end-to-end data flow in an IoT weather monitoring system. It shows the six key phases: sensor data collection, microcontroller processing, data transmission via protocols like MQTT, cloud storage, data analysis for weather patterns, and finally visualization on a dashboard. The loop represents continuous monitoring at regular sampling intervals.

examples.title: Python: Sensor Data Collection and MQTT Publishing
examples.description: Python implementation simulating sensor data collection and publishing to a cloud MQTT broker for IoT weather monitoring
examples.explanation: This Python implementation demonstrates the sensor data collection and MQTT publishing aspects of an IoT weather monitoring system. It includes a WeatherSensor class that simulates reading temperature, humidity, pressure, and wind speed values. The MQTTPublisher class simulates connecting to an MQTT broker and publishing weather data. The main loop continuously collects sensor readings and transmits them to the cloud, representing the real-world data flow from sensors through the microcontroller to the cloud platform. This shows how Python can be used for both simulation and actual IoT device programming on platforms like Raspberry Pi.

examples.title: C: ESP32 Microcontroller Sensor Data Acquisition
examples.description: C code for ESP32 microcontroller demonstrating sensor reading, data processing, and WiFi transmission for weather monitoring
examples.explanation: This C implementation demonstrates the microcontroller-side code that would run on an ESP32 or similar IoT microcontroller. It shows sensor initialization, reading raw ADC values from temperature/humidity/pressure/wind sensors, calibration to convert raw values to physical units, and WiFi/MQTT transmission to the cloud. The code structure mirrors real embedded IoT development with hardware abstraction for sensor reading, calibration functions for accuracy, and network transmission logic. This represents the 'brain' of the IoT system that processes raw sensor data before sending it to the cloud.

examples.title: Java: Spring Boot Cloud Backend for Weather Data
examples.description: Java Spring Boot application demonstrating cloud-side data reception, storage, analysis, and REST API for weather monitoring
examples.explanation: This Java Spring Boot implementation represents the cloud backend component of the IoT weather monitoring system. It demonstrates data ingestion from IoT devices via REST API, in-memory data storage simulating a database, weather pattern analysis that generates alerts for extreme conditions (high temperature, storms, high winds), and REST endpoints for data retrieval and statistics. This completes the full IoT data pipeline from sensor to cloud processing to visualization-ready endpoints, showing how the cloud platform analyzes data to predict weather patterns and detect anomalies as described in the module.
